content:
# Final Review Planner Positions
**Producer:** agent:claude/claude-opus-4-6

## Summary

Evaluated four amendments against the actual codebase. Two amendments identify real correctness/safety issues (terminal replay missing started_at guard, and merge logic vulnerable to silent field truncation). One amendment proposes a speculative optimization self-described as non-blocking. One amendment conflicts with intentional, well-documented design decisions in the codebase.

## Votes

- `fr-6-7bc10021`: Reject
  The amendment self-describes as 'a low-priority observation, not a blocking issue.' The owned-String approach was a deliberate design choice to unify the serialization and deserialization types (StartJournalDetails / CompletionJournalDetails) so the field set cannot drift between model and adapter layers. Introducing Cow<'a, str> would add lifetime complexity to these structs for a hypothetical future hot-path that does not exist today. The allocation cost of a few small String copies per journal event is negligible. This is a speculative optimization without a demonstrated need.
- `fr-6-bcd2cab6`: Accept
  This is a real data integrity bug. The open-match path (fs.rs:3028-3030) explicitly guards runless-to-named backfills with `entries[*index].started_at == started_at` and returns a descriptive error when they don't match (fs.rs:3039-3050). However, the terminal fallback via `unique_terminal_replay_match` (fs.rs:2756-2790) performs no started_at check at all — it only filters on terminal status and run_id presence. This means a replay of a finalized attempt with a mismatched started_at will silently backfill run_id onto the wrong terminal row, violating the disambiguation invariant the open-match path was built to enforce. The fix is straightforward: pass started_at into unique_terminal_replay_match and add the same guard, plus a regression test.
- `fr-6-279a800b`: Reject
  The code explicitly and deliberately skips snapshot-based plan_hash backfill for existing rows, with clear documentation at two locations (fs.rs:2903-2907 and fs.rs:3233-3237). The rationale is sound: persist_plan() can advance snapshot.plan_hash after the original attempt started, so the current snapshot hash may correspond to a newer plan version than what the attempt actually ran under. Stamping it would mislabel the attempt. This is a correctness-preserving design choice — having plan_hash: None (unlabeled) is strictly safer than having a potentially wrong plan_hash. The amendment assumes the snapshot hash is always 'canonical' for the attempt, but the code comments explain exactly why that assumption is unsafe in replay/repair scenarios where the snapshot may have changed between the original event and the repair.
- `fr-6-926ed414`: Accept
  This is a valid structural maintenance hazard. The merge functions in fs.rs (StartJournalDetails::merge at 2284-2305 and CompletionJournalDetails::merge at 2322-2354) hand-enumerate each optional field for conflict detection and backfill. The shared model structs use #[serde(deny_unknown_fields)] which protects deserialization, but the merge logic has no compile-time or test-time coverage ensuring all fields are handled. If a new optional field is added to the shared structs, the code compiles, round-trip serde tests pass, but append_event_if_missing will silently drop the new field during journal repair — a silent data loss scenario in a repair/recovery codepath where correctness matters most. A merge-coverage test (e.g., round-tripping a struct with all fields populated through merge and verifying none are lost) is low-cost and closes this gap.
